supporting_evidence: Known bicuspid aortic valve — strongest congenital risk factor for aortic dissection
Ascending aortic root dilated to 4.2 cm with lapsed follow-up surveillance (>2 years overdue)
Poorly controlled hypertension for 25 years — chronic aortic wall stress and medial degeneration
Simultaneous transient neurological deficit and melena could represent multi-territory malperfusion
Former 40-pack-year smoker — additional vascular wall damage
Classic presentation: transient visual disturbance and confusion resolving within minutes
Multiple vascular risk factors: age, hypertension, diabetes, former smoker
Fully resolved symptoms — consistent with TIA rather than completed stroke
ABCD2 risk stratification needed to guide urgency of workup
Melena (dark/black stools) consistent with upper GI haemorrhage
Aspirin 75 mg daily — direct mucosal toxicity risk
Mild tachycardia (HR 98) may indicate compensatory response to blood loss
Age and comorbidities increase bleeding severity risk
25-year history of poorly controlled hypertension
Transient neurological symptoms could represent hypertensive encephalopathy
Current BP 152/88 is elevated but may not fully represent peak (measured post-symptom resolution)
Target organ damage assessment warranted (renal function, fundoscopy)
Melena can result from mesenteric ischaemia causing mucosal breakdown
If aortic dissection is present, celiac/SMA involvement would cause mesenteric hypoperfusion
Elderly patient with extensive vascular disease burden
Lactate level would help assess for tissue ischaemia

reasoning: The simultaneous presentation of transient neurological symptoms and melena in a patient with known aortic pathology (bicuspid valve + dilated root) demands that aortic dissection be the primary diagnostic consideration. While TIA and upper GI bleed individually are more common diagnoses, the COINCIDENCE of both appearing simultaneously should raise suspicion for a unifying aortic process. The patient's history of poorly controlled hypertension, former heavy smoking, and critically lapsed aortic surveillance further elevate the pre-test probability. Bilateral arm blood pressure measurement is an essential first step — a differential >20 mmHg systolic would strongly support the diagnosis. CTA aorta should be obtained urgently if clinical suspicion persists.
